planting.note: Plant divisions or container stock in spring in saturated to moist, acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to part shade; water hyssop thrives in shallow water (1–4 inches), at pond margins, in rain garden depressions, and in consistently moist to wet garden beds — it requires consistently moist to wet conditions.

directions: Plant in saturated to moist, acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; water hyssop is an aquatic or semi-aquatic plant that thrives in shallow water, at pond margins, and in consistently wet garden beds — it will not thrive in dry or even moderately dry conditions. Bacopa monnieri (water hyssop or brahmi) is a creeping, aquatic to semi-aquatic herb native to wetlands across tropical and subtropical regions worldwide, including the southeastern United States; in Ayurvedic medicine, brahmi is one of the most revered medicinal herbs, used for centuries as a cognitive enhancer and adaptogen to support memory, concentration, and anxiety reduction — modern pharmacological research has partially supported these traditional uses, identifying bacosides as the active compounds. The fleshy, succulent, oblong leaves and small, white to pale-blue 4-petaled flowers identify the plant in the wild; in cultivation it is grown in water gardens, at pond margins, and in constantly moist beds. In zone 9 gardens it grows as a year-round perennial in moist areas and may naturalize. The botanical Bacopa monnieri is completely different from the ornamental Sutera cordata (also marketed as 'Bacopa' — see separate entry).
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Aquatic Plants

planting.note: Plant purchased transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; ornamental bacopa (Sutera) is a cool-to-mild-season annual or tender perennial that blooms most heavily in spring and fall in zone 9 and struggles in summer heat — it is best used as a cool-season annual or in shaded locations in warm climates.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; in zone 9, plant in fall or early spring for best bloom before summer heat; cut back lightly after the spring bloom flush to encourage new growth. Sutera cordata (ornamental bacopa or snowflake) is a South African tender perennial grown as a cool-season annual in most climates, producing masses of tiny, 4-petaled, white (or pink or lavender) flowers on a trailing, mounding plant with small, rounded, bronze-green, hairy leaves — the overall effect is a frothy cascade of tiny flowers that are exceptional in hanging baskets, window boxes, and as container spillers. NOTE: Despite being commonly marketed as 'Bacopa,' Sutera cordata is entirely unrelated to the medicinal herb Bacopa monnieri (water hyssop), which is an aquatic plant in the family Plantaginaceae — the shared common name is a marketing confusion. Sutera cordata performs best in cool to mild weather (55–75°F), blooming prolifically in spring and fall in zone 9 and going largely dormant in summer heat. It combines beautifully with petunias, calibrachoa, and lobularia in cool-season combination containers.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Container Gardening

common_name: Wheat Celosia (Flamingo Feather, Plumosa Spike Celosia)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 2–4 weeks before last frost (celosia dislikes transplanting — use peat pots to minimize root disturbance) or direct sow after soil warms to 70°F; wheat celosia thrives in hot, humid conditions and is outstanding for zone 9 summer and early fall gardens.

directions: Start seeds indoors 2–4 weeks before transplanting in peat pots, or direct sow after soil warms; plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil. Celosia spicata (wheat celosia) is distinct from the more familiar plumed celosia (C. argentea var. plumosa) and cockscomb (C. argentea var. cristata) in producing elegant, tapering, wheat-head-like flower spikes 3–4 inches long on upright, branching stems — the spikes are deep rose-pink to magenta-pink aging to silvery-white, creating a two-toned, naturalistic effect that looks right in both cottage-garden and cutting-garden contexts. The 'Flamingo Feather' series is the most widely grown cultivar group, producing uniform, graceful spikes excellent for cutting and drying. Unlike plumed celosias, wheat celosia has a lighter, more elegant texture that mixes naturally with other flowers rather than dominating the composition. It is drought-tolerant once established, thrives in summer heat, and provides long-lasting cut flowers that hold their color when dried. Zone 9: sow in March for summer bloom, again in August for fall color.

common_name: White Wood Sorrel (White Oxalis, Regnell Wood Sorrel)

planting.note: Plant small bulbs or divisions in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full shade; Oxalis regnellii is an outstanding shade-tolerant bulb for indoor and outdoor use, producing clean, clover-like foliage and white flowers over many months; in zones 9–11, it is perennial outdoors.

directions: Plant small, rhizome-like bulbs 1–2 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full shade; keep evenly moist during active growth. Oxalis regnellii (white wood sorrel) is a South American native producing distinctive three-leaflet, triangular, clean-green leaves (like giant clover) on delicate petioles and small, white, 5-petaled flowers on slender scapes — the combination of precise geometric foliage and delicate white flowers above it creates a refined, elegant effect perfect for shaded garden edges and container arrangements. Unlike the more familiar purple-leaved Oxalis triangularis ('purple shamrock'), O. regnellii has bright, clean green foliage that is cooler and more restrained in its effect, yet equally attractive. It grows from small, segmented, rhizome-like tubers that multiply into colonies over time; in zone 9, it is perennial outdoors in protected shaded spots, going dormant in extreme cold or heat and resprouting from tubers. It is also outstanding as a houseplant or shaded patio container plant, blooming through much of the year indoors. The foliage closes at night and reopens in morning light — a 'sleep movement' (nyctinasty) characteristic of the Oxalis genus.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Bulbs

common_name: Petai (Stink Bean, Bitter Bean, Twisted Cluster Bean)

planting.note: Plant container-grown seedlings in well-drained, deep, fertile, moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in frost-free zones 10–12; petai is a tall tropical legume tree that requires many years to fruit — it is rarely grown outside of its native Southeast Asian range except in southern Florida, the Caribbean, and similar frost-free climates.

directions: Plant in deep, fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in frost-free tropical conditions; petai requires a long growing season with warm temperatures year-round. Parkia speciosa (petai or stink bean) is a large, tropical legume tree of Southeast Asia producing distinctive, long (12–18 inch), flat, twisted seed pods containing 10–18 large, flat, bright-green seeds with an intensely pungent, sulfurous odor (similar to but stronger than garlic and fenugreek combined) that is a celebrated ingredient in Malaysian, Indonesian, and Thai cooking. The seeds are eaten raw, stir-fried with shrimp, added to curries and sambals, or mixed with garlic, chili, and shrimp paste — those accustomed to the flavor consider it a prized delicacy despite (or because of) its extreme pungency. Like asparagus with certain compounds, petai causes urine to smell strongly of its sulfurous compounds for several hours after eating — considered a normal, if somewhat inconvenient, effect. The tree is a nitrogen-fixer (fixing atmospheric nitrogen through root nodules) and has been used in agroforestry systems throughout Malaysia and Indonesia. Rarely available in the continental US except in specialty tropical fruit nurseries.
sources: FAO — Underutilized Crops of Southeast Asia
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Legumes
